phenomena: Bubbles of gas (CO2) are produced in the test tube.
Limewater turns cloudy due to the formation of insoluble CaCO3 precipitate.
A light blue precipitate forms and the solution turns cloudy, indicating the formation of copper(II) hydroxide.
Upon heating, liquid water boils and turns into invisible water vapor. When the vapor contacts a cold surface, it condenses into tiny droplets, appearing as mist or fog on the glass.
Upon heating in a hot water bath, solid paraffin gradually melts and transforms into liquid paraffin. No chemical change occurs; only a physical state change is observed.
Upon adding FeCl3 saturated solution to boiling distilled water, a reddish-brown Fe(OH)3 colloid forms, turning the liquid red-brown. The colloid scatters red laser light, showing the Tyndall effect.
Sodium moves rapidly on the water surface, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas. The reaction is vigorous, and heat may ignite the hydrogen, producing a yellow flame. The solution becomes alkaline.
Sodium reacts with ethanol, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas. The reaction is less vigorous than with water, and usually no flame is observed. The solution becomes alkaline.
In oxygen, the burning wood glows brighter and burns more vigorously, producing carbon dioxide gas.
Combustion of carbon (wood) in oxygen produces carbon dioxide and heat; the flame intensifies.
Complete combustion of wood (containing carbon and hydrogen) in oxygen produces carbon dioxide and water vapor.
In carbon dioxide, the burning wood is extinguished; carbon dioxide does not support combustion.
NaCl dissolves in water to form a colorless solution. The solution conducts electricity and lights the bulb.
KNO3 dissolves in water to form a colorless solution. The solution conducts electricity and lights the bulb.
A white precipitate forms upon mixing, indicating the formation of insoluble barium sulfate.
The clear limewater becomes less cloudy or remains clear; no precipitate forms. The solution remains colorless and transparent.
Bubbles of gas (CO2) are produced in the test tube, indicating effervescence.

After mixing, the blue color of the solution may become lighter or shift toward greenish-blue due to formation of CuCl2. No precipitate forms. Upon heating, the color change becomes more apparent, and the solution remains clear.
Detergent dissolves in water, forming a foamy cleaning solution.
Dirt and residues are emulsified and suspended in the detergent solution, visible as foam and cloudiness.
Exothermic neutralization reaction; temperature of the mixture increases, solution remains colorless.
Crude benzoic acid dissolves in hot distilled water. Upon cooling, purified benzoic acid crystallizes out, while insoluble impurities are removed by filtration.
Bubbling and gas evolution; acetylene gas is produced.

equation: 2 KMnO4(aq, acidic) + 5 C2H2(g) + 2 H2SO4(aq) → 2 MnSO4(aq) + 2 K2SO4(aq) + 10 CO2(g) + 8 H2O(l)
phenomena: Purple solution decolorizes as acetylene reduces permanganate.
Brown color of bromine disappears as acetylene reacts.
Acetylene burns with a luminous, sooty flame.
The purple litmus solution turns red in white vinegar (acidic).
The purple litmus solution turns blue in soap solution (basic).
Phenolphthalein remains colorless in white vinegar (acidic).
Phenolphthalein turns pink in soap solution (basic).
Phenolphthalein turns pink in weakly basic/neutral NaCl solution.
Upon addition of dilute HCl, the pink color fades and the solution becomes colorless as phenolphthalein turns colorless in acidic medium.
Effervescence observed as CO2 gas is produced; when CO2 is bubbled into limewater, a white precipitate of CaCO3 forms, turning the limewater cloudy.
Effervescence observed as CO2 gas is produced; when CO2 is bubbled into limewater, a white precipitate of CaCO3 forms, turning the limewater cloudy.
A white precipitate of CaCO3 forms in the limewater, making it cloudy.
A white precipitate forms and the solution becomes cloudy, indicating the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate.
A blue precipitate of Cu(OH)2 forms.
A white precipitate of BaSO4 forms.
Hydrochloric acid releases HCl gas, which has a pungent odor. When exposed to air containing ammonia, white fumes of NH4Cl may form near the bottle mouth.
White fumes (NH4Cl) appear when HCl vapor meets ammonia in the air.
The paper turns black where sulfuric acid was applied, indicating charring due to dehydration. The writing appears as black marks.
The surface of the wood turns black where sulfuric acid was applied, indicating charring and decomposition.
The cloth turns black and may shrink or decompose where sulfuric acid was applied, indicating charring and dehydration.
Exothermic dissolution; temperature rises significantly; solution remains colorless and clear.
When electric current passes through dilute hydrochloric acid, the solution conducts electricity, the indicator lamp lights up, hydrogen gas is produced at the cathode, and chlorine gas at the anode.

When electric current passes through dilute sulfuric acid, the solution conducts electricity, the indicator lamp lights up, hydrogen gas is produced at the cathode, and oxygen gas at the anode. The sulfuric acid acts as an electrolyte and is not consumed.

equation: 2 H2O(l) → 2 H2(g) + O2(g) (very slow, negligible conductivity)
phenomena: Distilled water conducts electricity extremely poorly; the indicator lamp does not light up, and almost no gas is produced.
Ethanol does not conduct electricity; the indicator lamp does not light up, and no gas is produced.
NaCl dissolves in water, forming a clear, colorless solution. No visible gas or precipitate forms. The temperature may change slightly depending on the amount dissolved.
Calcium chloride dissolves in water, forming a clear, colorless solution. The process is exothermic (releases heat).
Iron nail slowly reacts with dilute sulfuric acid, producing pale green solution and bubbles of hydrogen gas.
Iron nail reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid, producing pale green solution and bubbles of hydrogen gas.
Iron nail reacts slowly with dilute nitric acid, producing pale green solution and hydrogen gas. (Note: With concentrated HNO3, passivation occurs.)
Iron nail placed in copper sulfate solution becomes coated with reddish copper, solution fades from blue to pale green.
Iron nail placed in zinc sulfate solution shows no visible change.
Iron nail placed in aluminum sulfate solution shows no visible change.
Iron nail placed in magnesium sulfate solution shows no visible change.
Iron nail placed in copper(II) chloride solution becomes coated with reddish copper, solution fades from blue-green to pale green.
Iron nail placed in zinc chloride solution shows no visible change.
Iron nail placed in aluminum chloride solution shows no visible change.
Iron nail placed in magnesium chloride solution shows no visible change.
Copper piece placed in dilute sulfuric acid shows no visible change.
Copper piece placed in dilute hydrochloric acid shows no visible change.
Zinc granule reacts with dilute sulfuric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas.
Zinc granule reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas.
Aluminum piece reacts with dilute sulfuric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas.
Aluminum piece reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas.
Magnesium strip reacts vigorously with dilute sulfuric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas.
Magnesium strip reacts vigorously with dilute hydrochloric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas.
Graphite block placed in dilute sulfuric acid shows no visible change.
Graphite block placed in dilute hydrochloric acid shows no visible change.
Two red clay spheres are connected by a toothpick to form a model of a hydrogen molecule.
Two green clay spheres are connected by a toothpick to form a model of a chlorine molecule.
One white clay sphere is connected to two red clay spheres by two toothpicks at an angle of 105°, forming a water molecule model.
One black clay sphere is connected linearly to two white clay spheres by two toothpicks, forming a carbon dioxide molecule model.
One white clay sphere is connected to three red clay spheres by three toothpicks in a trigonal pyramidal geometry, forming an ammonia molecule model.
One black clay sphere is connected to four red clay spheres by four toothpicks in a tetrahedral geometry, forming a methane molecule model.
The freshly cut sodium surface is shiny and metallic, but quickly turns dull and gray as it reacts with oxygen in the air to form sodium oxide.
The glowing wood splint bursts into flame when inserted into the oxygen gas, indicating rapid combustion and the presence of oxygen.
After adding iron powder to the mixed_solution, the yellow color fades as Fe^{3+} is reduced to colorless Fe^{2+}.
After adding KSCN_solution_1M to the mixed_solution, a deep red color appears due to formation of [Fe(SCN)]^{2+} complex.

In the tea test tube, the purple color of acidic KMnO4 rapidly fades and disappears, indicating reduction of KMnO4 by tea polyphenols. In the distilled water test tube, the purple color remains, showing no reaction.

When pressure increases (volume decreases), the equilibrium shifts toward N2O4_gas, causing the color to fade (becomes lighter). When pressure decreases (volume increases), the equilibrium shifts toward NO2_gas, causing the color to intensify (reddish-brown).

Charcoal burns with a faint, reddish glow in air (limited oxygen), producing little light and heat. In pure oxygen, charcoal burns much more vigorously, with a brighter, whiter flame and faster combustion, producing carbon dioxide gas.
Two layers form initially; after shaking, the mixture becomes warm and may show partial mixing. Benzenesulfonic acid forms in the acid layer.
Two layers form initially; after shaking, the mixture becomes warm and may show partial mixing. Toluenesulfonic acid forms in the acid layer.
Two clear layers form; benzene floats on water.
Two clear layers form; toluene floats on water.
Metallic sodium burns in air with a bright yellow flame, producing white solid sodium oxide.

The gas mixture changes color depending on temperature: in hot water, the gas appears darker brown due to higher NO₂ concentration; in ice water, the gas becomes much lighter or nearly colorless due to increased N₂O₄ formation.
Effervescence observed as oxygen gas is released, relights glowing splint. The test tube feels warm (exothermic reaction). The solution turns pH paper blue, indicating strong alkalinity.

Na2CO3 dissolves in water, temperature may slightly decrease due to endothermic dissolution; forms a clear, colorless solution. When phenolphthalein is added, solution turns pink, indicating strong alkalinity.

NaHCO3 dissolves in water, temperature may slightly decrease due to endothermic dissolution; forms a clear, colorless solution. When phenolphthalein is added, solution remains colorless or turns very pale pink, indicating weak alkalinity.
The red-hot iron wire burns brightly with sparks in oxygen, forming brownish-red solid iron(III) oxide.
Upon heating NaHCO3, gas bubbles are produced. When the gas is bubbled into limewater, the limewater turns cloudy due to the formation of CaCO3 precipitate.
Limewater turns cloudy as white CaCO3 precipitate forms when CO2 is bubbled through.
Upon heating Na2CO3, no visible gas is produced and limewater remains clear, indicating no CO2 is released.
Upon heating potassium permanganate, it decomposes to form potassium manganate (green solid), manganese dioxide (brown-black solid), and oxygen gas (colorless, collected by water displacement).
Effervescence observed due to CO2 gas evolution; solution remains colorless.
Zinc reacts with dilute sulfuric acid, producing bubbles of colorless hydrogen gas and forming a colorless zinc sulfate solution.
Hydrogen gas burns with a bright flame in chlorine gas, producing dense white fumes of hydrogen chloride gas.

equation: No chemical reaction (O2 does not react with dry or wet colored paper, cloth, or fresh flower under these conditions)
phenomena: No visible color change occurs in dry or wet colored paper/cloth or fresh flower when exposed to dry oxygen at room temperature. The experiment demonstrates that oxygen alone does not cause fading or oxidation of dyes in these materials without additional factors (e.g., moisture, light, catalysts).
A white precipitate forms (AgCl).
A pale yellow precipitate forms (Ag2CO3).
The pale yellow precipitate dissolves and bubbles of gas (CO2) are observed.
The white precipitate (AgCl) does not dissolve in dilute nitric acid.
A white precipitate forms (AgCl).
Zinc granules react with sulfuric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas and forming a colorless solution of zinc sulfate. The reaction rate increases with higher acid concentration.
Zinc granules react more rapidly with concentrated sulfuric acid, producing hydrogen gas at a faster rate and forming zinc sulfate solution.
Sodium moves rapidly on water, melts into a sphere, produces bubbles of hydrogen gas, and the solution turns pink/red due to phenolphthalein indicating the formation of a base.
NO2 is brown, N2O4 is colorless. At lower temperatures, equilibrium shifts to N2O4 (colorless, lighter color); at higher temperatures, shifts to NO2 (brown, deeper color).
Solid NaCl dissolves completely in water, forming a clear, colorless solution.
After heating and mixing, two layers form. The upper aqueous layer contains ethanol and sodium bromide.
A pale yellow precipitate of silver bromide (AgBr) forms upon addition of silver nitrate.
When iron is added to hydrochloric acid solution, bubbles of hydrogen gas form rapidly on the iron surface, and the iron gradually dissolves.
When iron is added to acetic acid solution, a small amount of bubbles (hydrogen gas) form slowly on the iron surface, and the reaction proceeds much more slowly than with hydrochloric acid.
Solid fuchsin slowly dissolves and diffuses in water, forming a pink-purple solution without stirring.
A red-brown precipitate of Fe(OH)3 forms.
A pale green precipitate of Fe(OH)2 forms, which gradually turns red-brown due to oxidation in air.
The pale green Fe(OH)2 precipitate is oxidized by air to red-brown Fe(OH)3.

Bubbles of carbon dioxide gas are produced in the test tube, indicating a gas-evolving reaction. The reaction is not extremely vigorous, reflecting the weak acidity of acetic acid compared to carbonic acid.
FeCl3 solution turns blood-red upon addition of KSCN solution due to formation of Fe(SCN)3.
FeCl2 solution shows no obvious color change upon addition of KSCN solution.
Solid CuSO4 dissolves in water, forming a bright blue solution.
Solid CuCl2·2H2O dissolves in water, forming a blue-green solution.
Solid CuBr2 dissolves in water, forming a pale blue solution.
Solid NaCl dissolves in water, forming a colorless solution.
Solid K2SO4 dissolves in water, forming a colorless solution.
Solid KBr dissolves in water, forming a colorless solution.
Solid Mg(OH)2 dissolves, solution becomes clear, and no gas is produced.

equation: K2Cr2O7(aq) + 4 H2SO4(aq) + 3 C2H5OH(l) → Cr^{3+}(aq) + 3 CH3CHO(aq) + K2SO4(aq) + 7 H2O(l)
phenomena: Solution color changes from orange to blue-green, indicating oxidation of ethanol.
Iron powder reacts with ferric chloride solution; yellow color fades as FeCl3 is reduced to FeCl2.
Addition of KSCN to FeCl2 solution produces ferrous thiocyanate, resulting in a red color.
Addition of chlorine water oxidizes Fe2+ to Fe3+, solution turns yellow.
Addition of KSCN to FeCl3 solution produces ferric thiocyanate, deep blood-red color appears.
Addition of ammonia to Cu2plus_solution forms a blue precipitate of Cu(OH)2.
Excess ammonia dissolves the blue precipitate, forming a deep blue solution of tetraamminecopper(II) complex.
Addition of ethanol does not cause precipitation or layering; the solution remains homogeneous and deep blue.
Upon mixing, a new product is formed in solution. Observable phenomena (color change, precipitation, gas evolution) are not specified.
The solution turns blood-red, indicating the formation of [Fe(SCN)]^{2+} complex ion.
After mixing NaOH solution and dilute HCl, the solution becomes neutral; no visible change, but heat may be released.
Bubbles of gas are produced in the test tube. When a burning wood splint is brought near the mouth of the test tube, a 'pop' sound is heard, indicating the presence of hydrogen gas.

When the oxide layer is removed from the aluminum, bubbles of gas (H2) are rapidly produced. The gas can reignite a glowing splint, confirming it is hydrogen. If the oxide layer is present, little or no reaction is observed.
A white precipitate forms in the solution, indicating the formation of 2,4,6-tribromophenol. The reddish-brown color of bromine water fades.
The white AgCl precipitate gradually dissolves upon addition of ammonia solution, forming a colorless solution.
Upon mixing, the solution turns purple due to formation of the iron(III)-phenol complex.

Upon addition of ammonia to silver nitrate solution, a brown precipitate of Ag2O first forms, which dissolves upon further addition of ammonia, yielding a colorless diamminesilver(I) complex solution.

equation: 2 [Ag(NH3)2]NO3(aq) + CH3CHO(l) + 3 H2O(l) → 2 Ag(s) + CH3COONH4(aq) + 4 NH3(aq) + 2 HNO3(aq)
phenomena: Upon heating with acetaldehyde, a silver mirror forms on the inner wall of the test tube, and the solution remains colorless.
Blue Cu(OH)2 precipitate forms upon mixing.

equation: 2 Cu(OH)2(s) + CH3CHO(aq) + NaOH(aq) → Cu2O(s) + CH3COONa(aq) + 3 H2O(l)
phenomena: Upon heating and shaking, blue precipitate turns brick-red, indicating Cu2O formation.
On heating 1-bromobutane with ethanol, colorless gas (1-butene) is evolved. The gas decolorizes purple acidic KMnO4 solution, indicating the presence of an alkene.

equation: 3 C4H8 + 2 KMnO4 + 4 H2SO4 → 3 C4H8O2 + 2 Mn^{2+} + K2SO4 + 4 H2O
phenomena: The purple color of acidic KMnO4 fades as it oxidizes 1-butene, forming colorless Mn^{2+} ions.
Solid NaCl dissolves in water, forming a colorless neutral solution.
Solid Na2CO3 dissolves in water, forming a colorless basic solution.
Solid NH4Cl dissolves in water, forming a colorless acidic solution.
Solid KNO3 dissolves in water, forming a colorless neutral solution.
Solid CH3COONa dissolves in water, forming a colorless basic solution.
Solid (NH4)2SO4 dissolves in water, forming a colorless acidic solution.
Partial hydrolysis of Fe2(SO4)3 in water produces a reddish-brown Fe(OH)3 precipitate and increases solution acidity (lower pH).
Dilute sulfuric acid dissociates completely in water, increasing the concentration of H+ ions and lowering the pH.
Addition of acid (H+) dissolves Fe(OH)3 precipitate, shifting equilibrium and increasing Fe3+ in solution.
Bubbles of CO2 gas are rapidly produced; solution may effervesce vigorously.
Bubbles of CO2 gas are produced; effervescence observed, but less vigorous than with formic acid.
Bubbles of CO2 gas are produced slowly; effervescence is weak.
Bubbles of CO2 gas are produced; effervescence observed.
Bubbles of CO2 gas are produced; effervescence observed.
Bubbles of CO2 gas are produced slowly; weak effervescence.
No visible gas; acid-base reaction occurs, but no effervescence.
No visible gas; acid-base reaction occurs, but no effervescence.
No visible gas; acid-base reaction occurs, but no effervescence.
Hydrolysis of ethyl acetate in acidic or neutral conditions produces ethanol and acetic acid. The solution becomes more acidic over time, causing the pink color of phenolphthalein to fade.
Saponification (alkaline hydrolysis) of ethyl acetate produces ethanol and sodium acetate. As NaOH is consumed, the solution becomes less basic, causing the pink color of phenolphthalein to fade.
Acetic acid produced during hydrolysis reacts with sodium hydroxide, forming sodium acetate and water. This neutralizes the solution, further causing the phenolphthalein color to disappear.
Neutralization of hydrochloric acid by sodium hydroxide produces sodium chloride and water.
Potassium alum dissolves in water to form a colorless solution; undissolved solid indicates saturation.
Upon cooling, large transparent potassium alum crystals form from the saturated solution.
A purple chrome alum layer forms on the surface of the potassium alum crystal.
A new transparent potassium alum layer forms over the chrome alum coating, creating a composite crystal.
Upon addition of ammonia to copper(II) sulfate solution, the color changes from pale blue to deep blue due to formation of the tetraamminecopper(II) complex.
A white precipitate of silver chloride forms upon mixing silver nitrate and sodium chloride solutions.
Addition of potassium thiocyanate to ferric sulfate solution produces a blood red color due to formation of ferric thiocyanate complex.
Mixing ferric sulfate and potassium ferricyanide solutions produces a deep blue precipitate of Prussian blue.

equation: C2H5OH + Na2CO3(aq, sat) + CH3COOH → CH3COOC2H5 + H2O + NaHCO3
phenomena: Upon heating ethanol with acetic acid in the presence of saturated sodium carbonate solution, a fruity-smelling oil (ethyl acetate) forms and separates as the upper layer. Effervescence may be observed due to CO2 evolution if acid is added to carbonate.
The solution changes from red to colorless at the endpoint due to the indicator; neutralization occurs with formation of salt and water.
No obvious color change or precipitate; ethanol does not react with CuSO4 under these conditions.
Effervescence; bubbles of CO2 gas are produced.

equation: CH3CHO + 2 [Ag(NH3)2]OH (aq) + 3 H2O → CH3COOH + 2 Ag_silver_mirror + 4 NH3 + 2 H2O
phenomena: Silver mirror forms on the inner wall of the test tube.

equation: C6H10 + 2 KMnO4 (aq, acidic) + 2 H2SO4 → C6H10O2 + 2 MnO2_precipitate + 2 K2SO4 + 2 H2O
phenomena: Purple color of KMnO4 disappears; brown MnO2 precipitate forms.
Solution turns purple or blue-violet due to complex formation.
FeCl3 crystals dissolve in water, forming a yellow-brown solution.
Addition of concentrated HCl increases acidity; FeCl3 remains dissolved, no precipitate forms.
Soil suspension remains dispersed; FeCl3 may act as a coagulant, causing some particles to settle.
Al2(SO4)3 acts as a coagulant, causing soil particles to aggregate and settle.
Upon heating, FeCl3 hydrolyzes to form a reddish-brown Fe(OH)3 colloid in water.

equation: Fe(OH)3(colloid) + Na2CO3(aq) → Fe(OH)3(s) + NaCl(aq) + CO2(g) + H2O(l)
phenomena: Fe(OH)3 colloid aggregates and precipitates; solution clears, CO2 bubbles may be observed.
After adding chlorine water to KBr solution, the solution turns orange-brown due to the formation of bromine.
After adding chlorine water to KI solution, the solution turns brown due to the formation of iodine.
After adding bromine water to KI solution, the solution turns brown due to the formation of iodine.
After adding and dissolving alum powder in natural water, suspended particles aggregate and settle as flocs, resulting in clearer water and visible sediment at the bottom.
A silver mirror forms on the inner wall of the test tube, indicating the reduction of Ag+ to metallic silver by glucose.
A red precipitate of copper(I) oxide (Cu2O) forms, indicating the reduction of Cu2+ by glucose.
At the cathode (negative electrode), reddish copper metal is deposited. At the anode (positive electrode), pale green chlorine gas is evolved, which turns moist potassium iodide-starch paper blue.

equation: Protein (in cotton) + CuSO4·5H2O(aq) + NaOH(aq) → [Cu(Protein)(OH)2] (aq) + Na2SO4(aq) + H2O
phenomena: Upon heating, a purple color appears in the mixture, indicating the formation of the biuret complex and the presence of protein.

When the sample liquid is poured through the wetted filter paper, suspended solids are trapped on the filter paper as residue, while the filtrate collected in the beaker becomes clearer, indicating removal of impurities.
Iron nails react with oxygen and water, forming rust (hydrated iron(III) oxide); the volume of gas in the test tube decreases, causing the water column to rise.

Iron displaces copper from copper(II) sulfate solution; reddish copper deposits on the iron nail, and the solution may turn pale green due to iron(II) sulfate formation. Corrosion of iron is accelerated.
A white, cloudy precipitate forms upon addition of egg white solution to saturated ammonium sulfate, indicating protein precipitation (salting out).
Upon addition of distilled water and shaking, the precipitate dissolves or becomes less visible, and the solution clears up as the protein redissolves.

Zinc reacts with dilute sulfuric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas. When the hydrogen gas is ignited, it burns with a pale blue flame. Holding a dry beaker above the flame results in the formation of water droplets on the inner wall of the beaker.
Iron electrode (anode) gradually dissolves; may see slight color change near electrode.
Hydrogen gas bubbles form at the zinc electrode (cathode).
When potassium ferricyanide is added to the solution containing Fe^{2+}, a deep blue precipitate (Turnbull's blue) forms.
Upon heating, the egg white solution forms a white precipitate due to protein denaturation.
Addition of silver nitrate to egg white solution produces a grayish-white precipitate (silver-protein complex).
Addition of ethanol to egg white solution causes protein denaturation and white precipitate formation.
Addition of distilled water does not dissolve the silver-protein complex precipitate.
Addition of distilled water does not dissolve the denatured protein precipitate.
The solution turns yellow or orange-yellow due to the nitration of aromatic amino acids (xanthoproteic reaction).
Potassium burns with a lilac flame, producing white solid potassium oxide and releasing heat.
Potassium floats and moves rapidly on water, melts into a silvery ball, produces bubbles of hydrogen gas, and the solution turns pink with phenolphthalein, indicating it is alkaline.
Few bubbles (H2) form slowly at room temperature; white Mg(OH)2 precipitate forms; phenolphthalein turns faint pink due to weakly basic solution.
Bubbles (H2) increase rapidly; more white Mg(OH)2 precipitate forms; pink color intensifies with phenolphthalein.
White gelatinous Al(OH)3 precipitate forms upon addition of water to AlCl3 solution.
White Al(OH)3 precipitate dissolves, forming a colorless solution.

equation: Al(OH)3 (s) + MgCl2 (aq) → No visible reaction (Al(OH)3 remains insoluble)
phenomena: White Al(OH)3 precipitate remains; no color change or dissolution.
A silver mirror forms on the inner wall of the test tube.
A brick-red precipitate forms (Cu2O).
A blue gelatinous precipitate forms (Cu(OH)2).
Sucrose is hydrolyzed to glucose and fructose (not distinguished here).
No brick-red precipitate forms; solution remains blue.
After hydrolysis and alkalization, a brick-red precipitate forms (Cu2O).
No brick-red precipitate forms; solution remains blue.
Starch is hydrolyzed to glucose.
After hydrolysis and alkalization, a brick-red precipitate forms (Cu2O).
The colorless NaBr solution turns orange-brown after adding chlorine water, indicating the formation of bromine.
The colorless NaI solution turns brown after adding chlorine water, indicating the formation of iodine.
The colorless NaI solution turns brown after adding bromine water, indicating the formation of iodine.
Sodium reacts violently with water, producing hydrogen gas (bubbles), heat, and a colorless solution of sodium hydroxide.
Magnesium reacts very slowly with cold water, producing a small amount of hydrogen gas and a white precipitate of magnesium hydroxide.
Magnesium reacts more readily with hot water/steam, producing hydrogen gas and a white precipitate of magnesium hydroxide.
Aluminum reacts with sodium hydroxide solution, producing hydrogen gas and a colorless solution of sodium aluminate.
White precipitate of magnesium hydroxide dissolves, forming a colorless solution.
White precipitate of aluminum hydroxide dissolves, forming a colorless solution.
White precipitate of aluminum hydroxide dissolves in sodium hydroxide, forming a colorless solution.
Iron reacts with hydrochloric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas and forming a pale green iron(II) chloride solution.
Rust (iron(III) oxide) dissolves in hydrochloric acid, forming yellow-brown iron(III) chloride solution and water.
If iron is immersed in copper(II) sulfate solution without electricity, copper is deposited on iron and the solution turns pale green due to iron(II) sulfate.

equation: CuSO4(aq) + Fe(s) → Cu(s) + FeSO4(aq)  (Electroplating: at cathode)
phenomena: During electroplating, copper ions are reduced and deposited as a reddish-brown layer on the iron-nickel piece.

equation: 2H2O(l) → 2H2(g) + O2(g)  (electrolysis at graphite electrodes in Na2SO4_solution)
phenomena: Gas bubbles form at both electrodes: hydrogen at cathode, oxygen at anode. Near cathode, solution turns pink due to NaOH formation (phenolphthalein indicator). Near anode, solution remains colorless or may become slightly acidic due to H2SO4 formation.
When SO2 gas dissolves in water, the liquid level in the test tube rises. The resulting solution is acidic, as indicated by pH test paper.

equation: n C6H5OH (s) + n CH2O (aq) + HCl (aq) → (C6H4OH)n(CH2)n (s) [Novolac_Resin] + n H2O (l)
phenomena: Under acidic conditions, phenol and formaldehyde react to form a reddish-brown novolac resin. The mixture thickens and solidifies upon heating.

equation: n C6H5OH (s) + n CH2O (aq) → (C6H4OH)n(CH2)n (s) [Phenol_Formaldehyde_Resin_Base] + n H2O (l)
phenomena: Under neutral or weakly basic conditions, phenol and formaldehyde react to form a pale yellow to light brown phenol-formaldehyde resin. The product is less colored and may be softer compared to the acid-catalyzed resin.
The magenta color of fuchsin fades and the solution becomes colorless upon addition and mixing. Upon heating, the colorless solution remains unchanged, indicating reduction of fuchsin.
Magnesium strip burns with a bright white light, producing white powdery magnesium oxide.
Copper wire reacts with concentrated sulfuric acid upon heating, producing blue copper(II) sulfate solution, sulfur dioxide gas (which decolorizes fuchsin solution), and water.
A white precipitate forms (BaSO4).
A white precipitate forms (BaCO3).
The white precipitate dissolves and bubbles of gas (CO2) are produced.
The white precipitate (BaSO4) does not dissolve.
After adding air and shaking, the colorless NO gas turns into reddish-brown NO2 gas.
NO2 dissolves in water, the reddish-brown color fades, and acidic solution forms.
Ammonia gas rapidly dissolves in water, lowering the pressure in the flask and causing water to be drawn in (fountain effect).

equation: Phenolphthalein_Solution (C20H14O4 in H2O, aq) + Ammonia_Solution (NH3(aq)) → Phenolphthalein_in_Ammonia_Solution (C20H14O4 in NH3(aq), aq)
phenomena: Phenolphthalein turns red in the basic ammonia solution, indicating a high pH.
Upon heating, pungent ammonia gas is released, which turns moist red litmus paper blue.
Upon heating, pungent ammonia gas is released, which turns moist red litmus paper blue.
Upon heating, pungent ammonia gas is released, which turns moist red litmus paper blue.
Copper wire reacts with sulfuric acid, turning blue at the contact point (CuSO4 formation), and bubbles of SO2 gas are produced, which can be detected in the NaOH solution as gas bubbles.
Formation of white BaSO4 precipitate upon addition of BaCl2 solution to crude salt solution containing SO4^2-.
Formation of white CaCO3 precipitate upon addition of saturated Na2CO3 solution to crude salt solution containing Ca^2+.
Upon heating and evaporation, purified sodium chloride crystallizes as white solid.
A colorless gas with a rotten egg smell (H2S) is produced.

equation: 5 Na2S(aq) + 2 KMnO4(aq) + 3 H2SO4(aq) → K2SO4(aq) + 2 MnSO4(aq) + 5 S(s) + 5 H2O(l) + 5 Na2SO4(aq)
phenomena: Purple color of KMnO4 disappears, yellow sulfur precipitate forms.
Colorless SO2 gas is produced, blue CuSO4 solution forms.
Magenta fuchsin solution is decolorized.
The red color of the water fades as the red dye is adsorbed onto the activated carbon, resulting in clearer water and darkened carbon.
Bubbles of gas (hydrogen) are produced on the iron strip; the solution temperature increases slightly.
The mixture absorbs heat (endothermic), causing the temperature to drop and freezing water between the beaker and wood. Ammonia gas is released, detectable by its pungent smell.

equation: charcoal_powder (C, s) + 2 copper_oxide_powder (2 CuO, s) → 2 copper_powder (2 Cu, s) + carbon_dioxide (CO2, g)
phenomena: The black mixture turns reddish-brown as copper metal forms. Gas bubbles pass into limewater, turning it cloudy due to formation of calcium carbonate.

equation: carbon_dioxide (CO2, g) + limewater (Ca(OH)2(aq)) → calcium_carbonate (CaCO3, s) + H2O (l)
phenomena: Limewater turns cloudy as a white precipitate of calcium carbonate forms.

When zinc is immersed in dilute sulfuric acid, bubbles of hydrogen gas are produced on the zinc surface. If the circuit is completed with copper and zinc electrodes, a current is detected and gas evolution increases.
CO2 gas dissolves into water, reducing the gas pressure in the bottle and causing the bottle to collapse.
Carbon dioxide dissolves in water to form carbonic acid, making the solution weakly acidic.
Purple litmus turns red in the presence of carbonic acid (acidic conditions).
Red litmus paper exposed to air and dried may revert to purple as CO2 and H2CO3 dissipate.
Zinc dissolves, bubbles of hydrogen gas are produced on the zinc surface.
When zinc and copper electrodes are both inserted into dilute sulfuric acid and connected externally, the current flows, and hydrogen gas bubbles form on the copper electrode.
When zinc and graphite electrodes are both inserted into dilute sulfuric acid and connected externally, the current flows, and hydrogen gas bubbles form on the graphite electrode.
Zinc dissolves gradually, bubbles of hydrogen gas form on the zinc surface, and an electric current is generated when connected in a circuit with a suitable second electrode (Cu or graphite).

equation: 2 H2O2(aq) → 2 H2O(l) + O2(g)   (catalyzed by MnO2 or FeCl3)
phenomena: Rapid formation of oxygen bubbles; solution may become cloudy due to catalyst particles. Reaction rate increases with catalyst presence and higher concentration.
The mixture heats up (exothermic reaction), no visible gas is produced, and a white suspension may form.
In the presence of light, the mixture reacts: white fumes (HCl) are observed, and the volume of gas decreases. In the dark (aluminum foil wrapped), little or no reaction occurs.
Methane burns with a pale blue flame. Water droplets condense on the inner wall of the beaker. The gas produced can turn limewater milky.
Limewater turns milky due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate precipitate.
The orange color of the bromine solution gradually fades to colorless as ethylene reacts with bromine to form 1,2-dibromoethane.
Sodium reacts vigorously with anhydrous ethanol, producing sodium ethoxide (white solid) and hydrogen gas (bubbles). The hydrogen gas can be collected and ignited, burning with a pale blue flame.
When carbon dioxide is bubbled into limewater, a white precipitate of calcium carbonate forms, turning the solution cloudy.
The black copper(II) oxide coating on the copper wire is reduced, and the wire regains its metallic reddish color. The liquid in the test tube emits a pungent, fruity odor (acetaldehyde).

equation: C2H5OH + CH3COOH -> CH3COOC2H5 + H2O   (catalyzed by H2SO4)
phenomena: Upon heating, a colorless, oily liquid (ethyl acetate) forms and is observed as oil droplets in the sodium carbonate solution, accompanied by effervescence due to CO2 release from acid neutralization.
Starch is hydrolyzed by dilute sulfuric acid upon heating, producing glucose. The mixture turns from cloudy to clear as starch is converted.
When sodium hydroxide is added to copper(II) sulfate solution, a light blue precipitate of copper(II) hydroxide forms.
When glucose is heated with copper(II) hydroxide, a brick-red precipitate of copper(I) oxide (Cu2O) forms, indicating the presence of reducing sugars.
When iodine solution is added to starch (mantou or potato), a blue-black color appears, indicating the presence of starch.
Eggshell fragments (CaCO3) react with acetic acid, producing bubbles of carbon dioxide gas and dissolving the solid.

equation: C2H5OH(l) + CH3COOH(l) ⇌ CH3COOC2H5(l) + H2O(l)   (catalyzed by H2SO4)
phenomena: Ethanol and acetic acid, in the presence of concentrated sulfuric acid and heat, produce ethyl acetate (fruity ester odor) and water; two layers may form after addition of sodium carbonate solution.
Acetic acid reacts with saturated sodium carbonate solution, producing bubbles of carbon dioxide gas and forming sodium acetate in solution.
Zinc reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas. The gas causes a 'pop' sound when a burning splint is brought near.
Iron reacts slowly with dilute hydrochloric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas. The gas causes a 'pop' sound with a burning splint.
Magnesium reacts vigorously with dilute hydrochloric acid, producing many bubbles of hydrogen gas. The gas causes a loud 'pop' with a burning splint.
Copper does not react with dilute hydrochloric acid; no bubbles are observed.
Zinc reacts with dilute sulfuric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas. The gas causes a 'pop' sound when a burning splint is brought near.
Iron reacts slowly with dilute sulfuric acid, producing bubbles of hydrogen gas. The gas causes a 'pop' sound with a burning splint.
Magnesium reacts vigorously with dilute sulfuric acid, producing many bubbles of hydrogen gas. The gas causes a loud 'pop' with a burning splint.
Copper does not react with dilute sulfuric acid; no bubbles are observed.
Aluminum wire surface becomes covered with reddish-brown copper; blue color of solution fades.
Copper wire surface becomes covered with gray-white silver; solution turns blue.
No visible change; copper wire and solution remain unchanged.
Iron nails exposed to water and oxygen develop reddish-brown rust (hydrated iron(III) oxide) on their surface.
Calcium chloride absorbs water vapor, dissolving to form a colorless aqueous solution.
Sucrose dissolves in water with stirring, forming a clear, uniform solution. The solid disappears and the liquid remains transparent.
Iodine slightly dissolves in water, forming a very pale yellow-brown solution; most iodine remains undissolved.
Iodine dissolves readily in gasoline, forming a violet solution.
Potassium permanganate dissolves in water, forming a purple solution.
Potassium permanganate does not dissolve in gasoline; purple solid particles remain suspended in the colorless liquid.

Red ink disperses in water to form a uniform red solution. When ethanol is added along the tube wall, two layers may briefly form due to density differences, but after gentle mixing, a homogeneous red solution is observed.
NaCl dissolves in water with slight temperature decrease (endothermic or nearly neutral). Solution remains colorless.
NH4NO3 dissolves in water with a noticeable temperature decrease (endothermic). Solution remains colorless.
NaOH dissolves in water with a noticeable temperature increase (exothermic). Solution remains colorless.
Solid sodium chloride dissolves in distilled water, forming a clear and colorless solution. If too much sodium chloride is added, some may remain undissolved at the bottom.

Potassium sulfate dissolves in distilled water to form a clear, colorless solution. If excess solid is added beyond solubility, undissolved solid remains. Upon heating, more potassium sulfate dissolves. Upon cooling, crystals may precipitate out.
Blue solid dissolves in water to form a clear blue solution. The color intensity depends on the amount of copper sulfate dissolved.
Solid sodium chloride dissolves in distilled water to form a clear, colorless 6% NaCl solution.
The crude salt mixture dissolves in water; sodium chloride forms a clear solution, while insoluble impurities remain as residue.
Upon heating and evaporation, sodium chloride crystallizes as white solid in the evaporating dish.
Upon heating, dark purple KMnO4 decomposes to green K2MnO4, brown-black MnO2, and releases colorless O2 gas. Oxygen bubbles are observed at the glass tube outlet.

Electrolysis of water produces hydrogen gas at the cathode (makes flame extinguish with a 'pop' sound) and oxygen gas at the anode (relights glowing wood splint). Gases are colorless and collected separately.
Effervescence is observed as carbon dioxide gas is produced when dilute hydrochloric acid reacts with limestone.
Limewater turns cloudy due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate precipitate when carbon dioxide is bubbled through it.
Paraffin burns in air, producing carbon dioxide and water vapor. The candle flame shows distinct layers, and white smoke (condensed vapor) appears when extinguished.
Carbon dioxide reacts with limewater solution, forming a white precipitate of calcium carbonate and water. Limewater turns milky.
After adding MnO2_powder to H2O2_5percent_solution, bubbles of O2_gas are rapidly produced. A glowing splint inserted into the test tube reignites, confirming the presence of oxygen.
Upon addition of sodium hydroxide solution to phenolphthalein solution, the colorless solution turns pink due to formation of the pink phenolphthalein anion.
Bubbles of gas form at both electrodes. The gas at the cathode (hydrogen) can ignite with a 'pop' when exposed to a flame, while the gas at the anode (oxygen) causes a glowing splint to relight.
The blue color of the CuSO4 solution fades, a reddish-brown copper layer forms on the iron wire, and the solution turns pale green. The total mass before and after the reaction remains unchanged.
Copper powder changes from red to black as it reacts with oxygen to form copper(II) oxide. The mass of the system increases due to the uptake of oxygen.
